**Security Rule:** Any request that arrives over plain HTTP must be permanently redirected to the equivalent HTTPS URL so users and clients never continue an exchange on an unencrypted channel.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The server answers HTTP requests with real content instead of redirecting, so a user who types a bare hostname or follows an old link transacts entirely over an unencrypted connection.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** An HTTP listener exists only to issue a permanent redirect to the matching https URL, so every real exchange happens over TLS.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.UseRouting();            // no https redirection
```
Secure:
```
app.UseHttpsRedirection();
app.UseRouting();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if request.scheme == "http": serveContent(request)
```
Secure:
```
if request.scheme == "http": redirect301("https://" + host + path)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
// no transport-guarantee constraint configured
```
Secure:
```
<security-constraint><user-data-constraint>
  <transport-guarantee>CONFIDENTIAL</transport-guarantee>
</user-data-constraint></security-constraint>
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.use((req, res, next) => next());   // http handled normally
```
Secure:
```
app.use((req, res, next) =>
    req.secure ? next()
               : res.redirect(301, "https://" + req.headers.host + req.url));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@app.before_request
def check(): pass            # http requests served as-is
```
Secure:
```
@app.before_request
def force_https():
    if not request.is_secure:
        return redirect(request.url.replace("http://", "https://"), 301)
```
embed_text:
Any request that arrives over plain HTTP must be permanently redirected to the equivalent HTTPS URL so users and clients never continue an exchange on an unencrypted channel.
The server answers HTTP requests with real content instead of redirecting, so a user who types a bare hostname or follows an old link transacts entirely over an unencrypted connection.
An HTTP listener exists only to issue a permanent redirect to the matching https URL, so every real exchange happens over TLS.

document:
**Security Rule:** Responses must carry a Strict-Transport-Security header with a long max-age and includeSubDomains so browsers refuse any future plaintext connection to the site and its subdomains.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** No HSTS header is sent, or it carries a very short max-age, so a browser will still attempt an initial plaintext request that an attacker can intercept and downgrade.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every HTTPS response includes a Strict-Transport-Security header with a max-age of at least one year and includeSubDomains, and the site is submitted to the browser preload list.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
// no UseHsts() in the pipeline
```
Secure:
```
app.UseHsts();   // configure HstsOptions: MaxAge 730 days, IncludeSubDomains
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
# response sent with no Strict-Transport-Security header
```
Secure:
```
setHeader("Strict-Transport-Security",
          "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload")
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
response.getWriter().write(body);     // no HSTS header
```
Secure:
```
response.setHeader("Strict-Transport-Security",
    "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
res.send(body);                       // no HSTS header
```
Secure:
```
app.use(helmet.hsts({ maxAge: 63072000, includeSubDomains: true,
                      preload: true }));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
resp = make_response(body)            # no HSTS header
```
Secure:
```
resp.headers["Strict-Transport-Security"] = \
    "max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload"
```
embed_text:
Responses must carry a Strict-Transport-Security header with a long max-age and includeSubDomains so browsers refuse any future plaintext connection to the site and its subdomains.
No HSTS header is sent, or it carries a very short max-age, so a browser will still attempt an initial plaintext request that an attacker can intercept and downgrade.
Every HTTPS response includes a Strict-Transport-Security header with a max-age of at least one year and includeSubDomains, and the site is submitted to the browser preload list.

document:
**Security Rule:** Servers and clients must negotiate only TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3 and must disable SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1, which contain known weaknesses.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The TLS stack is left at a default that still permits legacy protocol versions, so an attacker can force a downgrade to a version with exploitable cryptographic flaws.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A minimum protocol version of TLS 1.2 is set explicitly, with TLS 1.3 preferred, and every obsolete SSL or early TLS version is turned off.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions();   // OS default
```
Secure:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions {
    EnabledSslProtocols = SslProtocols.Tls12 | SslProtocols.Tls13 };
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tlsContext(minVersion=SSLv3)
```
Secure:
```
tlsContext(minVersion=TLS1_2)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
SSLContext ctx = SSLContext.getInstance("TLS");
```
Secure:
```
SSLContext ctx = SSLContext.getInstance("TLSv1.3");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
https.createServer({ key, cert }, app);       # default min version
```
Secure:
```
https.createServer({ key, cert, minVersion: "TLSv1.2" }, app);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS)        # legacy versions allowed
```
Secure:
```
ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
ctx.minimum_version = ssl.TLSVersion.TLSv1_2
```
embed_text:
Servers and clients must negotiate only TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3 and must disable SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1, which contain known weaknesses.
The TLS stack is left at a default that still permits legacy protocol versions, so an attacker can force a downgrade to a version with exploitable cryptographic flaws.
A minimum protocol version of TLS 1.2 is set explicitly, with TLS 1.3 preferred, and every obsolete SSL or early TLS version is turned off.

document:
**Security Rule:** TLS must be configured to offer only strong cipher suites that provide forward secrecy and authenticated encryption, and to reject export-grade, NULL, anonymous, RC4, and 3DES ciphers.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The cipher list is left at a permissive default or explicitly includes weak suites, so a connection can negotiate a cipher with no forward secrecy or with a broken algorithm.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The cipher suite list is restricted to modern AEAD suites using ephemeral key exchange, and weak or legacy ciphers are removed entirely from the allowed set.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions();   // all OS ciphers
```
Secure:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions {
    CipherSuitesPolicy = new CipherSuitesPolicy(new[] {
        TlsCipherSuite.TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 }) };
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tlsContext(ciphers="DEFAULT:!NONE")
```
Secure:
```
tlsContext(ciphers="ECDHE+AESGCM:ECDHE+CHACHA20")
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
engine.setEnabledCipherSuites(engine.getSupportedCipherSuites());
```
Secure:
```
engine.setEnabledCipherSuites(new String[]{
    "TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384" });
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
https.createServer({ key, cert, ciphers: "ALL" }, app);
```
Secure:
```
https.createServer({ key, cert, honorCipherOrder: true,
    ciphers: "TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384" }, app);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx.set_ciphers("ALL:@SECLEVEL=0")
```
Secure:
```
ctx.set_ciphers("ECDHE+AESGCM:ECDHE+CHACHA20")
```
embed_text:
TLS must be configured to offer only strong cipher suites that provide forward secrecy and authenticated encryption, and to reject export-grade, NULL, anonymous, RC4, and 3DES ciphers.
The cipher list is left at a permissive default or explicitly includes weak suites, so a connection can negotiate a cipher with no forward secrecy or with a broken algorithm.
The cipher suite list is restricted to modern AEAD suites using ephemeral key exchange, and weak or legacy ciphers are removed entirely from the allowed set.

document:
**Security Rule:** A TLS client must verify that the server certificate chains to a trusted authority, is within its validity period, and has not been revoked, and must never disable certificate verification.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A client disables or ignores certificate verification, so any attacker presenting a self-signed or forged certificate can transparently intercept the supposedly secure connection.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Certificate verification is left enabled with the system or a pinned trust store, so a connection fails closed whenever the server cannot present a valid, trusted certificate.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
handler.ServerCertificateCustomValidationCallback = (m, c, ch, e) => true;
```
Secure:
```
handler.ServerCertificateCustomValidationCallback = null;   // default checks
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tlsConnect(host, verifyCertificate=false)
```
Secure:
```
tlsConnect(host, verifyCertificate=true)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx.init(null, new TrustManager[]{ trustEverythingManager }, null);
```
Secure:
```
ctx.init(null, null, null);   // default JVM trust store, full validation
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const agent = new https.Agent({ rejectUnauthorized: false });
```
Secure:
```
const agent = new https.Agent({ rejectUnauthorized: true });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
requests.get("https://api.example.com", verify=False)
```
Secure:
```
requests.get("https://api.example.com", verify=True)
```
embed_text:
A TLS client must verify that the server certificate chains to a trusted authority, is within its validity period, and has not been revoked, and must never disable certificate verification.
A client disables or ignores certificate verification, so any attacker presenting a self-signed or forged certificate can transparently intercept the supposedly secure connection.
Certificate verification is left enabled with the system or a pinned trust store, so a connection fails closed whenever the server cannot present a valid, trusted certificate.

document:
**Security Rule:** A TLS client must confirm that the hostname it connected to matches a name in the certificate's subjectAltName, so a valid certificate for one host cannot be used to impersonate another.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client trusts any certificate that chains to a trusted authority without checking the hostname, so an attacker uses a legitimate certificate for a domain they control to intercept other hosts.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Hostname verification is enabled so the requested host must appear in the certificate's subjectAltName entries before the handshake is accepted.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
handler.ServerCertificateCustomValidationCallback = (m, c, ch, e) => true;
```
Secure:
```
handler.ServerCertificateCustomValidationCallback =
    HttpClientHandler.DangerousAcceptAnyServerCertificateValidator is null
    ? null : null;   // leave null so the name and chain are both checked
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tlsConnect(host, checkHostname=false)
```
Secure:
```
tlsConnect(host, checkHostname=true)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
conn.setHostnameVerifier((hostname, session) -> true);
```
Secure:
```
conn.setHostnameVerifier(HttpsURLConnection.getDefaultHostnameVerifier());
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tls.connect({ host, checkServerIdentity: () => undefined });
```
Secure:
```
tls.connect({ host });   // default checkServerIdentity enforces the name
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx.check_hostname = False
```
Secure:
```
ctx.check_hostname = True
```
embed_text:
A TLS client must confirm that the hostname it connected to matches a name in the certificate's subjectAltName, so a valid certificate for one host cannot be used to impersonate another.
The client trusts any certificate that chains to a trusted authority without checking the hostname, so an attacker uses a legitimate certificate for a domain they control to intercept other hosts.
Hostname verification is enabled so the requested host must appear in the certificate's subjectAltName entries before the handshake is accepted.

document:
**Security Rule:** Cookies that carry session or sensitive data must set the Secure attribute so the browser only ever transmits them over an encrypted HTTPS connection.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A session cookie is issued without the Secure attribute, so the browser will attach it to a plaintext HTTP request and an attacker on the network can capture it.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every sensitive cookie is set with the Secure attribute, and usually HttpOnly as well, so it is confined to encrypted connections.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Response.Cookies.Append("session", token);
```
Secure:
```
Response.Cookies.Append("session", token,
    new CookieOptions { Secure = true, HttpOnly = true });
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
setCookie("session", token)
```
Secure:
```
setCookie("session", token, secure=true, httpOnly=true)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Cookie c = new Cookie("session", token);
```
Secure:
```
Cookie c = new Cookie("session", token);
c.setSecure(true); c.setHttpOnly(true);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
res.cookie("session", token);
```
Secure:
```
res.cookie("session", token, { secure: true, httpOnly: true });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
resp.set_cookie("session", token)
```
Secure:
```
resp.set_cookie("session", token, secure=True, httponly=True)
```
embed_text:
Cookies that carry session or sensitive data must set the Secure attribute so the browser only ever transmits them over an encrypted HTTPS connection.
A session cookie is issued without the Secure attribute, so the browser will attach it to a plaintext HTTP request and an attacker on the network can capture it.
Every sensitive cookie is set with the Secure attribute, and usually HttpOnly as well, so it is confined to encrypted connections.

document:
**Security Rule:** An HTTPS page must load all of its scripts, stylesheets, images, and other resources over HTTPS, so no part of the page can be tampered with or stripped of its protection.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An HTTPS page references a script or stylesheet over plain HTTP, so an attacker can modify that active resource and undermine the security of the whole page.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every subresource is referenced with an https or protocol-relative secure URL, and a Content-Security-Policy directive blocks or upgrades any remaining insecure request.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
<script src="http://cdn.example.com/app.js"></script>
```
Secure:
```
<script src="https://cdn.example.com/app.js"></script>
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const s = document.createElement("script");
s.src = "http://cdn.example.com/app.js";
```
Secure:
```
const s = document.createElement("script");
s.src = "https://cdn.example.com/app.js";
```
embed_text:
An HTTPS page must load all of its scripts, stylesheets, images, and other resources over HTTPS, so no part of the page can be tampered with or stripped of its protection.
An HTTPS page references a script or stylesheet over plain HTTP, so an attacker can modify that active resource and undermine the security of the whole page.
Every subresource is referenced with an https or protocol-relative secure URL, and a Content-Security-Policy directive blocks or upgrades any remaining insecure request.

document:
**Security Rule:** TLS private keys must be generated with sufficient strength, stored with restrictive permissions or in a secret manager, and never committed to source control or shipped in build artifacts.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A TLS private key is checked into the repository or stored world-readable on disk, so anyone who reads it can impersonate the server or decrypt captured traffic.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The private key is loaded at runtime from a secret manager or a file with owner-only permissions, and the key material itself is kept out of version control and logs.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var cert = new X509Certificate2("server.pfx", "hardcoded-password");
```
Secure:
```
var cert = new X509Certificate2(
    Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("TLS_PFX_PATH"),
    secretManager.Get("tls/pfx-password"));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
key = readFile("./server.key")        # committed alongside the code
```
Secure:
```
key = secretManager.get("tls/server-key")
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ks.load(new FileInputStream("src/main/resources/keystore.jks"), pw);
```
Secure:
```
ks.load(new FileInputStream(System.getenv("TLS_KEYSTORE_PATH")),
        secretManager.get("tls/keystore-pw").toCharArray());
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx.load_cert_chain("server.crt", "committed_server.key")
```
Secure:
```
key_path = os.environ["TLS_KEY_PATH"]   # owner-only file outside the repo
ctx.load_cert_chain(os.environ["TLS_CERT_PATH"], key_path)
```
embed_text:
TLS private keys must be generated with sufficient strength, stored with restrictive permissions or in a secret manager, and never committed to source control or shipped in build artifacts.
A TLS private key is checked into the repository or stored world-readable on disk, so anyone who reads it can impersonate the server or decrypt captured traffic.
The private key is loaded at runtime from a secret manager or a file with owner-only permissions, and the key material itself is kept out of version control and logs.

document:
**Security Rule:** When mutual TLS is used to authenticate clients, the server must require and verify a client certificate against a trusted authority, rather than treating client authentication as optional.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A server that relies on client certificates configures them as optional or skips the verification step, so a client with no valid certificate is still allowed to connect and is treated as trusted.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The server is set to require a client certificate and to reject the handshake when the presented certificate is missing, untrusted, or expired.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions {
    ClientCertificateRequired = false };
```
Secure:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions {
    ClientCertificateRequired = true };
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tlsServer(clientAuth="optional")
```
Secure:
```
tlsServer(clientAuth="required", clientCa=trustedCaBundle)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
sslEngine.setWantClientAuth(true);
```
Secure:
```
sslEngine.setNeedClientAuth(true);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tls.createServer({ key, cert, requestCert: true });
```
Secure:
```
tls.createServer({ key, cert, ca: clientCa,
                   requestCert: true, rejectUnauthorized: true });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_OPTIONAL
```
Secure:
```
ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED
ctx.load_verify_locations("client-ca.pem")
```
embed_text:
When mutual TLS is used to authenticate clients, the server must require and verify a client certificate against a trusted authority, rather than treating client authentication as optional.
A server that relies on client certificates configures them as optional or skips the verification step, so a client with no valid certificate is still allowed to connect and is treated as trusted.
The server is set to require a client certificate and to reject the handshake when the presented certificate is missing, untrusted, or expired.

document:
**Security Rule:** Connections to databases, caches, message brokers, and other backend services must be encrypted with TLS and verify the server certificate, just like public-facing connections.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A database or broker connection string disables SSL or sets the SSL mode to a non-verifying value, so backend credentials and query data cross the internal network in cleartext.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The connection requires TLS and verifies the server certificate against a configured authority, so backend traffic is encrypted and authenticated end to end.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var cs = "Host=db;Database=app;SSL Mode=Disable";
```
Secure:
```
var cs = "Host=db;Database=app;SSL Mode=VerifyFull;Root Certificate=ca.pem";
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
dbConnect(host, sslMode="disable")
```
Secure:
```
dbConnect(host, sslMode="verify-full", sslRootCert=caBundle)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var url = "jdbc:postgresql://db/app?ssl=false";
```
Secure:
```
var url = "jdbc:postgresql://db/app?ssl=true&sslmode=verify-full";
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
new Pool({ host: "db", ssl: false });
```
Secure:
```
new Pool({ host: "db", ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: true, ca: caPem } });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
psycopg.connect("host=db dbname=app sslmode=disable")
```
Secure:
```
psycopg.connect("host=db dbname=app sslmode=verify-full "
                 "sslrootcert=/etc/ssl/ca.pem")
```
embed_text:
Connections to databases, caches, message brokers, and other backend services must be encrypted with TLS and verify the server certificate, just like public-facing connections.
A database or broker connection string disables SSL or sets the SSL mode to a non-verifying value, so backend credentials and query data cross the internal network in cleartext.
The connection requires TLS and verifies the server certificate against a configured authority, so backend traffic is encrypted and authenticated end to end.

document:
**Security Rule:** Every piece of untrusted input must be checked for both correct syntax — the right type, format, and length — and correct semantics — a value that makes sense for its business purpose — before use.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Input is consumed directly from the request with no type, format, or range check, so malformed or out-of-range values flow into business logic and downstream sinks unchecked.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Input is parsed into a strongly typed value and checked against an expected format and a meaningful range or business constraint at the trust boundary, and rejected when it does not conform.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var qty = int.Parse(Request.Form["quantity"]);
```
Secure:
```
if (!int.TryParse(Request.Form["quantity"], out var qty) || qty < 1 || qty > 100)
    return BadRequest();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
age = request.get("age")
process(age)
```
Secure:
```
age = parseInteger(request.get("age"))
if age < 0 or age > 130: reject()
process(age)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
int qty = Integer.parseInt(request.getParameter("quantity"));
```
Secure:
```
int qty = Integer.parseInt(request.getParameter("quantity"));
if (qty < 1 || qty > 100) throw new ValidationException("quantity");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const qty = req.body.quantity;
```
Secure:
```
const qty = Number(req.body.quantity);
if (!Number.isInteger(qty) || qty < 1 || qty > 100) return res.sendStatus(400);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
quantity = request.form["quantity"]
```
Secure:
```
quantity = int(request.form["quantity"])
if not 1 <= quantity <= 100:
    abort(400)
```
embed_text:
Every piece of untrusted input must be checked for both correct syntax — the right type, format, and length — and correct semantics — a value that makes sense for its business purpose — before use.
Input is consumed directly from the request with no type, format, or range check, so malformed or out-of-range values flow into business logic and downstream sinks unchecked.
Input is parsed into a strongly typed value and checked against an expected format and a meaningful range or business constraint at the trust boundary, and rejected when it does not conform.

document:
**Security Rule:** Validation must be positive — accept only values that match a well-defined allowlist of known-good patterns or members — rather than trying to enumerate and block bad values.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Input is screened with a denylist that strips or rejects known-bad substrings, so any malicious payload not anticipated by the list passes through unblocked.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Input is compared against an explicit allowlist of permitted values or a tightly anchored pattern of permitted characters, and anything outside the allowlist is rejected outright.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var role = Request.Query["role"].ToString().Replace("'", "");
```
Secure:
```
var allowed = new[] { "viewer", "editor", "owner" };
if (!allowed.Contains(Request.Query["role"].ToString())) return BadRequest();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if "<script>" not in input: accept(input)
```
Secure:
```
if input in {"USD", "EUR", "GBP"}: accept(input)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String color = request.getParameter("color").replaceAll("[<>]", "");
```
Secure:
```
Set<String> allowed = Set.of("red", "green", "blue");
if (!allowed.contains(request.getParameter("color"))) throw new ValidationException();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const sort = req.query.sort.replace(/[;'"]/g, "");
```
Secure:
```
const allowed = ["name", "date", "price"];
if (!allowed.includes(req.query.sort)) return res.sendStatus(400);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
value = request.args["sort"].replace("--", "")
```
Secure:
```
if request.args["sort"] not in {"name", "date", "price"}:
    abort(400)
```
embed_text:
Validation must be positive — accept only values that match a well-defined allowlist of known-good patterns or members — rather than trying to enumerate and block bad values.
Input is screened with a denylist that strips or rejects known-bad substrings, so any malicious payload not anticipated by the list passes through unblocked.
Input is compared against an explicit allowlist of permitted values or a tightly anchored pattern of permitted characters, and anything outside the allowlist is rejected outright.

All security-relevant validation must be enforced on the server; client-side checks are a usability aid only and can never be trusted because the client is fully under attacker control.
The application relies on browser or mobile-client validation alone, so a request crafted outside the official client skips every check and reaches business logic with invalid data.
The server independently revalidates every field of every request regardless of any client-side validation, treating each request as if it could have been forged.

document:
**Security Rule:** Request bodies must be bound only to an explicit allowlist of fields a caller is permitted to set, so sensitive properties such as role, balance, or isAdmin can never be assigned from client input.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A whole request body is bound directly onto a domain or persistence object, so a caller can add unexpected fields and overwrite privileged properties that were never meant to be writable.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Input is bound to a narrow data transfer object exposing only the fields the endpoint permits, and that object is mapped field by field onto the domain entity.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
[HttpPost] public IActionResult Update(User user) { _db.Update(user); return Ok(); }
```
Secure:
```
[HttpPost] public IActionResult Update([Bind("Name,Email")] User user) {
    _db.Update(user); return Ok();
}
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
user.update(request.body)        # binds every field, including role
```
Secure:
```
user.name = request.body.name    # only allowlisted fields copied
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@PostMapping("/profile")
void update(@ModelAttribute User user) { repo.save(user); }
```
Secure:
```
@InitBinder
void bind(WebDataBinder b) { b.setAllowedFields("name", "email"); }
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Object.assign(user, req.body);
```
Secure:
```
const { name, email } = req.body;
Object.assign(user, { name, email });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
user = User(**request.json)
```
Secure:
```
user = User(name=request.json["name"], email=request.json["email"])
```
embed_text:
Request bodies must be bound only to an explicit allowlist of fields a caller is permitted to set, so sensitive properties such as role, balance, or isAdmin can never be assigned from client input.
A whole request body is bound directly onto a domain or persistence object, so a caller can add unexpected fields and overwrite privileged properties that were never meant to be writable.
Input is bound to a narrow data transfer object exposing only the fields the endpoint permits, and that object is mapped field by field onto the domain entity.

document:
**Security Rule:** Untrusted input must be canonicalized to a single normal form — consistent Unicode normalization and a single decoding pass — before it is validated, so the validated value equals the value used.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Input is validated in one representation but consumed in another, so an encoded or alternately normalized payload slips past a check that examined a different form of the same bytes.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Input is decoded and Unicode-normalized once at the trust boundary, and validation runs against that canonical form, which is then the only form passed downstream.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var f = Request.Query["file"].ToString();
if (!f.Contains("..")) Read(f);
```
Secure:
```
var f = Path.GetFullPath(Request.Query["file"]).Normalize(NormalizationForm.FormC);
if (f.StartsWith(BaseDir)) Read(f);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if isSafe(input): use(decode(input))
```
Secure:
```
canonical = normalize(decode(input))
if isSafe(canonical): use(canonical)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String f = request.getParameter("file");
if (!f.contains("..")) read(f);
```
Secure:
```
String f = Normalizer.normalize(
    Paths.get(request.getParameter("file")).normalize().toString(),
    Normalizer.Form.NFC);
if (!f.contains("..")) read(f);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const name = req.query.name;
if (allowed.has(name)) use(name);
```
Secure:
```
const name = decodeURIComponent(req.query.name).normalize("NFC");
if (allowed.has(name)) use(name);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
path = request.args["file"]
if ".." not in path: open(path)
```
Secure:
```
import unicodedata, os
path = unicodedata.normalize("NFC", os.path.normpath(request.args["file"]))
if ".." not in path: open(path)
```
embed_text:
Untrusted input must be canonicalized to a single normal form — consistent Unicode normalization and a single decoding pass — before it is validated, so the validated value equals the value used.
Input is validated in one representation but consumed in another, so an encoded or alternately normalized payload slips past a check that examined a different form of the same bytes.
Input is decoded and Unicode-normalized once at the trust boundary, and validation runs against that canonical form, which is then the only form passed downstream.

document:
**Security Rule:** Email addresses must be validated with a simple, conservative check and confirmed by sending a verification message, rather than rejected by an overly strict regular expression.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Email input is accepted with no structural check, or screened by a fragile pattern that both rejects valid addresses and accepts strings carrying header or injection characters.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The address is checked for one at-sign with non-empty local and domain parts and a sane length, has control characters rejected, and is then confirmed by a verification email before it is trusted.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (email.Contains("@")) Store(email);
```
Secure:
```
if (new EmailAddressAttribute().IsValid(email)) SendVerification(email);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if "@" in email: store(email)
```
Secure:
```
if isValidEmailShape(email) and noControlChars(email):
    sendVerification(email)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (email.contains("@")) store(email);
```
Secure:
```
if (EmailValidator.getInstance().isValid(email)) sendVerification(email);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (email.includes("@")) save(email);
```
Secure:
```
if (validator.isEmail(email) && !/[\r\n]/.test(email)) sendVerification(email);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
email = request.form["email"]
store(email)
```
Secure:
```
from email_validator import validate_email
email = validate_email(request.form["email"]).normalized
send_verification(email)
```
embed_text:
Email addresses must be validated with a simple, conservative check and confirmed by sending a verification message, rather than rejected by an overly strict regular expression.
Email input is accepted with no structural check, or screened by a fragile pattern that both rejects valid addresses and accepts strings carrying header or injection characters.
The address is checked for one at-sign with non-empty local and domain parts and a sane length, has control characters rejected, and is then confirmed by a verification email before it is trusted.

document:
**Security Rule:** Uploaded files must be validated by extension allowlist and verified content type, capped in size, stored under a generated name outside the web root, and served without being executed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An uploaded file is saved under its client-supplied name and extension inside an executable directory, so an attacker uploads a script and then requests it to run code on the server.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The upload is checked against an allowlist of extensions and a sniffed content type, size-limited, given a server-generated random name, and stored in a non-executable location outside the web root.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await file.CopyToAsync(File.Create("wwwroot/" + file.FileName));
```
Secure:
```
if (!Allowed.Contains(file.ContentType)) return BadRequest();
await file.CopyToAsync(File.Create(Path.Combine(UploadDir, $"{Guid.NewGuid()}.png")));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
save(upload.filename, upload.bytes)      # client name, web root
```
Secure:
```
if ext(upload) in ALLOWED and contentType(upload) in ALLOWED:
    save(randomName() + ext(upload), upload.bytes, dir=UPLOADS_OUTSIDE_WEBROOT)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Files.write(Path.of("webapp", file.getOriginalFilename()), file.getBytes());
```
Secure:
```
if (!ALLOWED_TYPES.contains(file.getContentType())) throw new ValidationException();
Files.write(uploadDir.resolve(UUID.randomUUID() + ".png"), file.getBytes());
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
fs.writeFileSync("public/" + file.originalname, file.buffer);
```
Secure:
```
if (!["image/png", "image/jpeg"].includes(file.mimetype)) return res.sendStatus(400);
fs.writeFileSync(path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, randomUUID() + ".png"), file.buffer);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
f = request.files["doc"]
f.save(os.path.join("static", f.filename))
```
Secure:
```
f = request.files["doc"]
if imghdr.what(f) not in {"png", "jpeg"}: abort(400)
f.save(os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, f"{uuid4()}.png"))
```
embed_text:
Uploaded files must be validated by extension allowlist and verified content type, capped in size, stored under a generated name outside the web root, and served without being executed.
An uploaded file is saved under its client-supplied name and extension inside an executable directory, so an attacker uploads a script and then requests it to run code on the server.
The upload is checked against an allowlist of extensions and a sniffed content type, size-limited, given a server-generated random name, and stored in a non-executable location outside the web root.

document:
**Security Rule:** Untrusted data must never be passed to a native object deserializer; use a data-only format such as JSON into a known target type, or restrict deserialization to an allowlist of expected classes.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Serialized bytes from an untrusted source are handed to a native deserializer that can instantiate arbitrary types, letting a crafted payload assemble a gadget chain and run code while it is reconstructed.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is parsed with a data-only format into a fixed, known data transfer type, and any unavoidable object deserialization is constrained by a strict type allowlist filter.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var o = new BinaryFormatter().Deserialize(stream);
```
Secure:
```
var o = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<KnownDto>(stream);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
obj = nativeDeserialize(untrustedBytes)
```
Secure:
```
obj = jsonParse(untrustedBytes, intoType=KnownDto)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Object o = new ObjectInputStream(in).readObject();
```
Secure:
```
ObjectInputStream ois = new ObjectInputStream(in);
ois.setObjectInputFilter(ObjectInputFilter.Config.createFilter("com.app.dto.*;!*"));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const obj = nodeSerialize.unserialize(req.body);
```
Secure:
```
const obj = JSON.parse(req.body);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
obj = pickle.loads(request.data)
```
Secure:
```
obj = OrderDto(**json.loads(request.data))
```
embed_text:
Untrusted data must never be passed to a native object deserializer; use a data-only format such as JSON into a known target type, or restrict deserialization to an allowlist of expected classes.
Serialized bytes from an untrusted source are handed to a native deserializer that can instantiate arbitrary types, letting a crafted payload assemble a gadget chain and run code while it is reconstructed.
Untrusted input is parsed with a data-only format into a fixed, known data transfer type, and any unavoidable object deserialization is constrained by a strict type allowlist filter.

document:
**Security Rule:** XML parsers handling untrusted documents must be configured to disable document type definitions and external entity resolution, so a malicious document cannot read files or trigger network requests.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An XML parser runs with its default configuration, so a document declaring an external or parameter entity makes the parser fetch local files, internal URLs, or recursively expand and exhaust memory.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The parser is hardened before any untrusted input — document type definitions disallowed, external general and parameter entities disabled — or a library that is XXE-safe by default is used.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var doc = new XmlDocument();
doc.LoadXml(untrustedXml);
```
Secure:
```
var doc = new XmlDocument { XmlResolver = null };
doc.LoadXml(untrustedXml);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
doc = xmlParser.parse(untrustedXml)        # DTD and entities resolved
```
Secure:
```
xmlParser.disallowDoctypeDecl()
doc = xmlParser.parse(untrustedXml)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
DocumentBuilderFactory dbf = DocumentBuilderFactory.newInstance();
```
Secure:
```
DocumentBuilderFactory dbf = DocumentBuilderFactory.newInstance();
dbf.setFeature("http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl", true);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const doc = libxmljs.parseXml(untrustedXml, { noent: true });
```
Secure:
```
const doc = libxmljs.parseXml(untrustedXml, { noent: false, nonet: true });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tree = xml.etree.ElementTree.parse(untrusted_xml)
```
Secure:
```
import defusedxml.ElementTree as ET
tree = ET.parse(untrusted_xml)
```
embed_text:
XML parsers handling untrusted documents must be configured to disable document type definitions and external entity resolution, so a malicious document cannot read files or trigger network requests.
An XML parser runs with its default configuration, so a document declaring an external or parameter entity makes the parser fetch local files, internal URLs, or recursively expand and exhaust memory.
The parser is hardened before any untrusted input — document type definitions disallowed, external general and parameter entities disabled — or a library that is XXE-safe by default is used.

document:
**Security Rule:** Numeric and string input must be checked against minimum and maximum bounds, so out-of-range numbers and oversized strings cannot drive overflow, resource exhaustion, or unexpected logic.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A number or string is accepted with no upper or lower bound, so an extreme value triggers an overflow, allocates excessive memory, or pushes business logic into an invalid state.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each numeric field is validated against an explicit allowed range and each string field against a maximum length before use, with out-of-bounds values rejected.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var limit = int.Parse(Request.Query["limit"]);
FetchRows(limit);
```
Secure:
```
if (!int.TryParse(Request.Query["limit"], out var limit) || limit is < 1 or > 100)
    limit = 20;
FetchRows(limit);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
allocate(request.get("count"))
```
Secure:
```
count = parseInteger(request.get("count"))
if 1 <= count <= 1000: allocate(count)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String bio = request.getParameter("bio");
repo.save(bio);
```
Secure:
```
String bio = request.getParameter("bio");
if (bio.length() > 2000) throw new ValidationException("bio too long");
repo.save(bio);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const limit = Number(req.query.limit);
fetchRows(limit);
```
Secure:
```
let limit = Number(req.query.limit);
if (!Number.isInteger(limit) || limit < 1 || limit > 100) limit = 20;
fetchRows(limit);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
page_size = int(request.args["size"])
rows = query(limit=page_size)
```
Secure:
```
page_size = int(request.args["size"])
page_size = max(1, min(page_size, 100))
rows = query(limit=page_size)
```
embed_text:
Numeric and string input must be checked against minimum and maximum bounds, so out-of-range numbers and oversized strings cannot drive overflow, resource exhaustion, or unexpected logic.
A number or string is accepted with no upper or lower bound, so an extreme value triggers an overflow, allocates excessive memory, or pushes business logic into an invalid state.
Each numeric field is validated against an explicit allowed range and each string field against a maximum length before use, with out-of-bounds values rejected.

document:
**Security Rule:** When validation fails the request must be rejected outright; the application must not silently coerce, truncate, or partially clean bad input and then continue processing it.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Invalid input is quietly repaired — stripped of offending characters or coerced to a default — and processing continues, so an attacker can shape input that survives the repair as a still-dangerous value.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A validation failure stops processing immediately with a clear rejection and a generic error response, and the invalid request is never used in any downstream operation.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var sku = Regex.Replace(Request.Form["sku"], "[^A-Z0-9]", "");
AddItem(sku);
```
Secure:
```
var sku = Request.Form["sku"].ToString();
if (!Regex.IsMatch(sku, "^[A-Z0-9]{8}$")) return BadRequest();
AddItem(sku);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
clean = stripBadChars(input)     # repair, then keep going
process(clean)
```
Secure:
```
if not isValid(input): reject()
process(input)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String slug = request.getParameter("slug").replaceAll("[^a-z-]", "");
publish(slug);
```
Secure:
```
String slug = request.getParameter("slug");
if (!slug.matches("[a-z][a-z-]{2,49}")) throw new ValidationException("slug");
publish(slug);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const code = req.body.code.replace(/\D/g, "");
redeem(code);
```
Secure:
```
const code = req.body.code;
if (!/^\d{6}$/.test(code)) return res.sendStatus(400);
redeem(code);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
username = re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9]", "", request.form["username"])
create_user(username)
```
Secure:
```
username = request.form["username"]
if not re.fullmatch(r"[a-z0-9]{3,20}", username):
    abort(400)
create_user(username)
```
embed_text:
When validation fails the request must be rejected outright; the application must not silently coerce, truncate, or partially clean bad input and then continue processing it.
Invalid input is quietly repaired — stripped of offending characters or coerced to a default — and processing continues, so an attacker can shape input that survives the repair as a still-dangerous value.
A validation failure stops processing immediately with a clear rejection and a generic error response, and the invalid request is never used in any downstream operation.

document:
**Security Rule:** A user-supplied filename must never be concatenated onto a base directory directly; resolve the combined path to its canonical form and confirm it still lies inside the intended base directory.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A request parameter is joined onto a server directory and opened without canonicalization, so dot-dot segments or an absolute path let an attacker read or write files outside the intended directory.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The combined path is resolved to its absolute canonical form and checked to confirm it still starts with the canonical base directory before the file is opened.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var path = Path.Combine(UploadDir, Request.Query["name"]);
return File(System.IO.File.ReadAllBytes(path), "application/octet-stream");
```
Secure:
```
var baseDir = Path.GetFullPath(UploadDir);
var path = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(baseDir, Request.Query["name"]));
if (!path.StartsWith(baseDir + Path.DirectorySeparatorChar)) return Forbid();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
path = baseDir + "/" + userFilename
open(path)
```
Secure:
```
resolved = canonicalize(join(baseDir, userFilename))
if not resolved.startsWith(canonicalize(baseDir)): reject()
open(resolved)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
File f = new File(UPLOAD_DIR, request.getParameter("name"));
return Files.readAllBytes(f.toPath());
```
Secure:
```
Path base = Paths.get(UPLOAD_DIR).toRealPath();
Path f = base.resolve(request.getParameter("name")).normalize();
if (!f.startsWith(base)) throw new SecurityException("traversal");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]

res.sendFile(file);

Secure:
```
const file = path.resolve(UPLOAD_DIR, req.query.name);
if (!file.startsWith(path.resolve(UPLOAD_DIR) + path.sep)) return res.sendStatus(403);
res.sendFile(file);
```

```

return open(path).read()
```

path = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(base, request.args["name"]))
if os.path.commonpath([base, path]) != base: abort(403)
```

The combined path is resolved to its absolute canonical form and checked to confirm it still starts with the canonical base directory before the file is opened.

**Security Rule:** The accepted type of an uploaded file must be confirmed from its actual content, such as magic bytes or a server-side type detector, and matched against an allowlist; the client-supplied extension and Content-Type header must never be trusted.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Upload validation relies only on the file extension or the browser-supplied Content-Type, so an attacker renames an executable or script and it passes the check.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The file content is inspected to detect its real type, the detected type is checked against an allowlist of permitted types, and the extension is then derived from that verified type.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (Path.GetExtension(file.FileName) == ".png") SaveFile(file);
```
Secure:
```
var inspector = new FileFormatInspector();
var fmt = inspector.DetermineFileFormat(file.OpenReadStream());
if (fmt is Png or Jpeg) SaveFile(file);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if userFilename.endsWith(".jpg"): save(upload)
```
Secure:
```
detected = detectTypeFromContent(upload.bytes)
if detected in ALLOWED_TYPES: save(upload)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (part.getSubmittedFileName().endsWith(".pdf")) save(part);
```
Secure:
```
String type = new Tika().detect(part.getInputStream());
if ("application/pdf".equals(type)) save(part);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (req.file.mimetype === "image/png") fs.writeFileSync(dest, req.file.buffer);
```
Secure:
```
const { fileTypeFromBuffer } = await import("file-type");
const t = await fileTypeFromBuffer(req.file.buffer);
if (t && ["image/png", "image/jpeg"].includes(t.mime)) fs.writeFileSync(dest, req.file.buffer);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if file.filename.endswith((".png", ".jpg")):
    file.save(dest)
```
Secure:
```
import magic
mime = magic.from_buffer(file.read(2048), mime=True)
if mime in {"image/png", "image/jpeg"}: file.save(dest)
```

Upload validation relies only on the file extension or the browser-supplied Content-Type, so an attacker renames an executable or script and it passes the check.
The file content is inspected to detect its real type, the detected type is checked against an allowlist of permitted types, and the extension is then derived from that verified type.

**Security Rule:** Uploaded files must be stored under a server-generated name, never the raw client-supplied filename, so attacker-controlled path segments, special characters, and double extensions cannot influence where or how the file is written.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client-supplied filename is used verbatim as the storage name, so embedded path separators, null bytes, or a misleading double extension change the write location or later interpretation.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A fresh random identifier is generated server-side for the stored file, the extension is taken from the verified content type, and the original name is kept only as a display label in metadata.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var dest = Path.Combine(UploadDir, file.FileName);
```
Secure:
```
var stored = $"{Guid.NewGuid():N}.{ExtFor(verifiedType)}";
var dest = Path.Combine(UploadDir, stored);
```

```

```

part.write(UPLOAD_DIR + "/" + part.getSubmittedFileName());
```

```

```
Secure:

fs.writeFileSync(path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, stored), req.file.buffer);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
file.save(os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, file.filename))
```
Secure:
```

file.save(os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, stored))
```

The client-supplied filename is used verbatim as the storage name, so embedded path separators, null bytes, or a misleading double extension change the write location or later interpretation.
A fresh random identifier is generated server-side for the stored file, the extension is taken from the verified content type, and the original name is kept only as a display label in metadata.

**Security Rule:** Uploaded files must be stored outside the web server document root, or in a location the server will not execute, so an uploaded script can never be requested and run as server-side code.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Uploads are written into a directory the web server serves and can execute, so an attacker uploads a script file and then requests its URL to run code on the server.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Uploads are written to a path outside the document root and served only through a controller that streams the bytes, with script execution disabled for any upload directory.

Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```

// FileResult action streams the file after an authorization check
```

```

```

```

```

```

```

Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```

# downloads go through an authenticated send_file controller
```

**Security Rule:** Every upload endpoint must enforce a maximum request and file size at the framework or server layer, so an oversized upload is rejected early and cannot exhaust memory or disk.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An upload handler reads the whole request body into memory or onto disk with no size cap, so a very large or endless upload exhausts memory, fills the disk, or stalls the worker.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A maximum content length is configured on the framework or server so requests exceeding it are rejected before the body is fully read or buffered.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
public IActionResult Upload(IFormFile file) { /* no limit */ }
```
Secure:
```
[RequestSizeLimit(10_485_760)]
public IActionResult Upload(IFormFile file) { }
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
body = readEntireBody(request)      # no size cap
```
Secure:
```
if contentLength(request) > MAX_UPLOAD: reject(413)
body = readEntireBody(request)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@MultipartConfig
public class UploadServlet extends HttpServlet { }
```
Secure:
```
@MultipartConfig(maxFileSize = 10_485_760, maxRequestSize = 10_485_760)
public class UploadServlet extends HttpServlet { }
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const upload = multer({ dest: "uploads/" });
```
Secure:
```
const upload = multer({ dest: "uploads/", limits: { fileSize: 10 * 1024 * 1024 } });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
data = request.files["f"].read()
```
Secure:
```
app.config["MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH"] = 10 * 1024 * 1024  # 413 past 10 MB
data = request.files["f"].read()
```

An upload handler reads the whole request body into memory or onto disk with no size cap, so a very large or endless upload exhausts memory, fills the disk, or stalls the worker.
A maximum content length is configured on the framework or server so requests exceeding it are rejected before the body is fully read or buffered.

**Security Rule:** When user-supplied files are served back, the response must set a Content-Disposition of attachment, send the verified content type rather than a guessed one, and send X-Content-Type-Options nosniff so the browser cannot reinterpret the file as active content.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A stored file is served inline with a missing or attacker-influenced content type and no nosniff header, so the browser sniffs it as HTML and runs embedded script as stored cross-site scripting.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Downloads are returned with Content-Disposition attachment, the server-verified content type, and X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, so the browser downloads the file instead of rendering it.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return File(fileBytes, "text/html");
```
Secure:
```
Response.Headers["X-Content-Type-Options"] = "nosniff";
return File(fileBytes, verifiedType, displayName);  // FileResult sends attachment
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
respond(body=fileBytes)             # inline, sniffable, no type
```
Secure:
```
respond(body=fileBytes, contentType=verifiedType,
        disposition="attachment", nosniff=true)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
response.getOutputStream().write(fileBytes);
```
Secure:
```
response.setContentType(verifiedType);
response.setHeader("X-Content-Type-Options", "nosniff");
response.setHeader("Content-Disposition", "attachment; filename=\"file\"");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
res.sendFile(filePath);
```
Secure:
```
res.set("X-Content-Type-Options", "nosniff");
res.type(verifiedType).attachment(displayName).sendFile(filePath);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return send_file(path)
```
Secure:
```
resp = send_file(path, mimetype=verified_type, as_attachment=True)
resp.headers["X-Content-Type-Options"] = "nosniff"
return resp
```
embed_text:

A stored file is served inline with a missing or attacker-influenced content type and no nosniff header, so the browser sniffs it as HTML and runs embedded script as stored cross-site scripting.
Downloads are returned with Content-Disposition attachment, the server-verified content type, and X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, so the browser downloads the file instead of rendering it.

**Security Rule:** When extracting an uploaded archive, each entry's destination path must be validated to stay inside the extraction directory, and the total entry count and uncompressed size must be capped to prevent Zip Slip path traversal and decompression-bomb attacks.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Archive entries are extracted using their stored names without checking the resolved path or the expanding size, so a crafted entry name escapes the directory or a tiny archive expands to exhaust disk.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each entry name is resolved against the extraction root and rejected if it escapes, and extraction stops once a cap on entry count or cumulative uncompressed bytes is reached.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
entry.ExtractToFile(Path.Combine(destDir, entry.FullName));
```
Secure:
```
var full = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(destDir, entry.FullName));
if (!full.StartsWith(destDir + Path.DirectorySeparatorChar) || entry.Length > Max)
    throw new IOException("unsafe entry");
entry.ExtractToFile(full);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for entry in archive: writeFile(join(destDir, entry.name), entry.data)
```
Secure:
```
target = canonicalize(join(destDir, entry.name))
if not target.startsWith(destDir) or totalBytes > MAX: reject()
writeFile(target, entry.data)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Path out = destDir.resolve(entry.getName());
Files.copy(zip, out);
```
Secure:
```
Path out = destDir.resolve(entry.getName()).normalize();
if (!out.startsWith(destDir) || entry.getSize() > MAX) throw new IOException("unsafe entry");
Files.copy(zip, out);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
with zipfile.ZipFile(upload) as z: z.extractall(dest)
```
Secure:
```
base = os.path.realpath(dest)
for m in z.infolist():
    p = os.path.realpath(os.path.join(base, m.filename))
    if not p.startswith(base + os.sep) or m.file_size > MAX: raise ValueError
    z.extract(m, base)
```
embed_text:
When extracting an uploaded archive, each entry's destination path must be validated to stay inside the extraction directory, and the total entry count and uncompressed size must be capped to prevent Zip Slip path traversal and decompression-bomb attacks.
Archive entries are extracted using their stored names without checking the resolved path or the expanding size, so a crafted entry name escapes the directory or a tiny archive expands to exhaust disk.
Each entry name is resolved against the extraction root and rejected if it escapes, and extraction stops once a cap on entry count or cumulative uncompressed bytes is reached.

Every download or file-access request must verify that the authenticated caller is permitted to access that specific file; presenting a valid file identifier must never be sufficient on its own to retrieve it.
A file is looked up and returned by an identifier taken from the request with no ownership or permission check, so an attacker enumerates or guesses identifiers to read other users' files.
The file record is loaded and an authorization check confirms the current user owns or is granted access to it before the bytes are streamed back.

**Security Rule:** When the application fetches a user-supplied URL, the parsed scheme must be checked against an allowlist of safe network protocols, so a file scheme or other local-resource scheme cannot be used to read files off the server.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A user-supplied URL is passed to a fetch routine that also honors the file scheme, so an attacker supplies a file URL and the routine returns the contents of a local file.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The URL is parsed and its scheme is compared against an allowlist of http and https, with file and other local schemes rejected before any fetch is attempted.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var body = await httpClient.GetStringAsync(userUrl);
```
Secure:
```
var u = new Uri(userUrl);
if (u.Scheme != Uri.UriSchemeHttp && u.Scheme != Uri.UriSchemeHttps)
    return BadRequest();
var body = await httpClient.GetStringAsync(u);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
content = fetch(userUrl)            # file scheme honored
```
Secure:
```
if scheme(userUrl) not in {"http", "https"}: reject()
content = fetch(userUrl)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
InputStream in = new URL(userUrl).openStream();
```
Secure:
```
URL u = new URL(userUrl);
if (!Set.of("http", "https").contains(u.getProtocol())) throw new IOException("scheme");
InputStream in = u.openStream();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const body = await fetch(req.query.url).then(r => r.text());
```
Secure:
```
const u = new URL(req.query.url);
if (u.protocol !== "http:" && u.protocol !== "https:") return res.sendStatus(400);
const body = await fetch(u).then(r => r.text());
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
data = urllib.request.urlopen(request.args["url"]).read()
```
Secure:
```
scheme = urlparse(request.args["url"]).scheme
if scheme not in ("http", "https"): abort(400)
data = requests.get(request.args["url"], timeout=5).content
```

A user-supplied URL is passed to a fetch routine that also honors the file scheme, so an attacker supplies a file URL and the routine returns the contents of a local file.
The URL is parsed and its scheme is compared against an allowlist of http and https, with file and other local schemes rejected before any fetch is attempted.

**Security Rule:** Filenames received from a user must be validated against a strict allowlist of characters and rejected if they contain null bytes, control characters, or path separators, before the value is used in any filesystem operation.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A filename is used in a filesystem path without rejecting embedded null bytes or separators, so a null byte truncates the path past an appended extension or a separator redirects the operation.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The filename is matched against a strict allowlist regular expression and any value containing a null byte, control character, or path separator is rejected before the path is built.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var path = Path.Combine(BaseDir, Request.Query["name"]);
```
Secure:
```
var name = Request.Query["name"].ToString();
if (!Regex.IsMatch(name, "^[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,64}$")) return BadRequest();
var path = Path.Combine(BaseDir, name);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:

```
Secure:

if not matches(userFilename, "^[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,64}$"): reject()

```

```
Secure:

Path p = Paths.get(BASE, name);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const file = path.join(BASE, req.query.name);
```
Secure:
```
if (!/^[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,64}$/.test(req.query.name)) return res.sendStatus(400);
const file = path.join(BASE, req.query.name);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```
name = request.args["name"]
if not re.fullmatch(r"[A-Za-z0-9_-]{1,64}", name): abort(400)
path = os.path.join(BASE, name)
```

A filename is used in a filesystem path without rejecting embedded null bytes or separators, so a null byte truncates the path past an appended extension or a separator redirects the operation.
The filename is matched against a strict allowlist regular expression and any value containing a null byte, control character, or path separator is rejected before the path is built.

**Security Rule:** Uploaded files must be quarantined and scanned by an anti-malware engine before they are made available for download or processing, so a hosted file cannot be used to distribute malware to other users.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An uploaded file is immediately moved into active storage and offered for download with no malware scan, so the application becomes a distribution point for an infected file.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The upload is written to a quarantine area, scanned by an anti-malware engine, and only promoted to active storage after the scan returns clean.

Vulnerable:
```
await file.CopyToAsync(System.IO.File.Create(Path.Combine(PublicDir, stored)));
```
Secure:
```
var q = Path.Combine(QuarantineDir, stored);
await file.CopyToAsync(System.IO.File.Create(q));
if ((await _antivirus.ScanAsync(q)).IsClean) System.IO.File.Move(q, Path.Combine(PublicDir, stored));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]

```

```
Secure:
```
saveToQuarantine(upload)
if antivirusScan(upload) == "clean": moveToPublicStorage(upload)
```

```
Files.copy(part.getInputStream(), Paths.get(PUBLIC_DIR, stored));
```
Secure:
```
Path q = Paths.get(QUARANTINE_DIR, stored);
Files.copy(part.getInputStream(), q);
if (antivirus.scan(q).isClean()) Files.move(q, Paths.get(PUBLIC_DIR, stored));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
file.save(os.path.join(PUBLIC_DIR, stored))
```
Secure:
```

if clamd.ClamdUnixSocket().scan(quarantine_path)[quarantine_path][0] == "OK":
    shutil.move(quarantine_path, os.path.join(PUBLIC_DIR, stored))
```

The upload is written to a quarantine area, scanned by an anti-malware engine, and only promoted to active storage after the scan returns clean.

**Security Rule:** Error responses returned to a client must be generic and free of internal detail; stack traces, exception messages, and source paths must never reach the user.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An unhandled exception is rendered straight into the HTTP response, so the caller receives a stack trace, a SQL error, or a file path that reveals the framework, schema, and internal layout.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Exceptions are caught at the boundary, logged in full on the server, and the client receives a short generic message and a correlation identifier with no internal detail.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
catch (Exception ex) { return Content(ex.ToString()); }
```
Secure:
```
catch (Exception ex) { _logger.LogError(ex, "failed");
  return Problem("Internal error"); }
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
catch (e) { respond(500, e.stackTrace) }
```
Secure:
```
catch (e) { log(e); respond(500, "An error occurred. Ref: " + id) }
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
catch (Exception e) { resp.getWriter().write(e.toString()); }
```
Secure:
```
catch (Exception e) { log.error("failed", e);
  resp.sendError(500, "Internal error"); }
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.use((err, req, res, next) => res.status(500).send(err.stack));
```
Secure:
```
app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
  logger.error(err); res.status(500).json({ error: "internal_error" });
});
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return Response(str(exc) + traceback.format_exc(), status=500)
```
Secure:
```
logger.exception("request failed")
return JSONResponse({"error": "internal_error"}, status_code=500)
```

Exceptions are caught at the boundary, logged in full on the server, and the client receives a short generic message and a correlation identifier with no internal detail.

**Security Rule:** Debug, development, and verbose-error modes must be disabled in production so the framework's interactive error pages and detailed diagnostics are never served to end users.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application runs in production with debug mode left on, so the framework serves interactive tracebacks, environment dumps, and in some frameworks a live console to anyone who triggers an error.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Debug mode is forced off in production builds and configuration, so failures fall through to a generic custom error page while full detail is written only to server-side logs.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();
```
Secure:
```
if (env.IsDevelopment()) app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();
else app.UseExceptionHandler("/error");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
config.debug = true        # left on in the production deployment
```
Secure:
```
config.debug = false       # generic error page in production
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
server.error.include-stacktrace=always
```
Secure:
```
server.error.include-stacktrace=never
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.set("env", "development");
```
Secure:
```
process.env.NODE_ENV = "production";  // detailed errors suppressed
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.run(debug=True)
```
Secure:
```
app.run(debug=False)       # DEBUG=False also set in settings
```
embed_text:
Debug, development, and verbose-error modes must be disabled in production so the framework's interactive error pages and detailed diagnostics are never served to end users.
The application runs in production with debug mode left on, so the framework serves interactive tracebacks, environment dumps, and in some frameworks a live console to anyone who triggers an error.
Debug mode is forced off in production builds and configuration, so failures fall through to a generic custom error page while full detail is written only to server-side logs.

document:
**Security Rule:** Security decisions must fail closed: when an authorization, validation, or verification step throws, the outcome must be denial, never an implicit grant.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A permission or token check is wrapped so that an exception is swallowed and execution continues, leaving an authorized flag at its permissive default and granting access on failure.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The security flag defaults to denied and is set to allowed only on an explicit successful check; any exception leaves it denied and the request is rejected.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
bool ok = true;
try { ok = Authz.Check(user); } catch { }
```
Secure:
```
bool ok = false;
try { ok = Authz.Check(user); } catch { ok = false; }
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
allowed = true
try { allowed = checkAccess(user) } catch (e) { /* ignored */ }
```
Secure:
```
allowed = false
try { allowed = checkAccess(user) } catch (e) { allowed = false }
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
boolean ok = true;
try { ok = authz.check(user); } catch (Exception e) { }
```
Secure:
```
boolean ok = false;
try { ok = authz.check(user); } catch (Exception e) { ok = false; }
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
let ok = true;
try { ok = await verify(token); } catch { /* swallow */ }
```
Secure:
```
let ok = false;
try { ok = await verify(token); } catch { ok = false; }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
try: ok = verify_token(t)
except Exception: ok = True
```
Secure:
```
try: ok = verify_token(t)
except Exception: ok = False
```
embed_text:
Security decisions must fail closed: when an authorization, validation, or verification step throws, the outcome must be denial, never an implicit grant.
A permission or token check is wrapped so that an exception is swallowed and execution continues, leaving an authorized flag at its permissive default and granting access on failure.
The security flag defaults to denied and is set to allowed only on an explicit successful check; any exception leaves it denied and the request is rejected.

document:
**Security Rule:** Logs must never contain passwords, session tokens, API keys, full payment card numbers, or other secrets; sensitive fields must be omitted or masked before any log call.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A whole request body, credential object, or header set is logged verbatim, so passwords, bearer tokens, and card numbers land in log files and any downstream log aggregator.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Only non-sensitive fields are logged, and known secret fields are redacted or replaced with a masked placeholder before the value reaches the logger.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
_logger.LogInformation("login {@Dto}", loginDto);
```
Secure:
```
_logger.LogInformation("login {User}", loginDto.Username);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
log.info("login attempt: " + credentials)
```
Secure:
```
log.info("login attempt for user: " + credentials.username)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
log.info("auth header: {}", request.getHeader("Authorization"));
```
Secure:
```
log.info("auth present: {}", request.getHeader("Authorization") != null);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
logger.info("body", req.body);
```
Secure:
```
logger.info("body", { ...req.body, password: "[REDACTED]" });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
logger.info("payload: %s", request.json)
```
Secure:
```
safe = {k: v for k, v in request.json.items() if k != "password"}
logger.info("payload: %s", safe)
```
embed_text:
Logs must never contain passwords, session tokens, API keys, full payment card numbers, or other secrets; sensitive fields must be omitted or masked before any log call.
A whole request body, credential object, or header set is logged verbatim, so passwords, bearer tokens, and card numbers land in log files and any downstream log aggregator.
Only non-sensitive fields are logged, and known secret fields are redacted or replaced with a masked placeholder before the value reaches the logger.

document:
**Security Rule:** Untrusted data written to logs must have carriage return and line feed characters removed or encoded so an attacker cannot forge log entries or break log parsing.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A user-supplied value containing newline characters is concatenated into a log message, so the attacker injects a fake second log line or corrupts a downstream log parser.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Newline and other control characters are stripped or encoded from untrusted values before they are logged, or a structured logger that encodes fields is used.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
_logger.LogInformation("user input: " + userInput);
```
Secure:
```
_logger.LogInformation("user input: {Input}",
    userInput.Replace("\r", "").Replace("\n", ""));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
log.info("user search: " + userInput)
```
Secure:
```
log.info("user search: " + stripCrlf(userInput))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
log.info("user input: " + userInput);
```
Secure:
```
log.info("user input: {}", userInput.replaceAll("[\\r\\n]", "_"));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
logger.info("search term: " + req.query.q);
```
Secure:
```
logger.info("search term: " + req.query.q.replace(/[\r\n]/g, ""));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
logger.info("login failed for %s", username)
```
Secure:
```
logger.info("login failed for %s",
            username.replace("\r", "").replace("\n", ""))
```
embed_text:
Untrusted data written to logs must have carriage return and line feed characters removed or encoded so an attacker cannot forge log entries or break log parsing.
A user-supplied value containing newline characters is concatenated into a log message, so the attacker injects a fake second log line or corrupts a downstream log parser.
Newline and other control characters are stripped or encoded from untrusted values before they are logged, or a structured logger that encodes fields is used.

Security-relevant events — authentication success and failure, access control denials, input validation failures, and privilege changes — must be logged with enough context to support detection and forensics.
Authentication failures, authorization denials, and other security events occur silently, so there is no record an attacker can be detected from and no audit trail after an incident.
Each security-relevant event is logged at an appropriate level with the actor, action, outcome, and a timestamp, using a consistent event vocabulary across the application.

document:
**Security Rule:** Caught exceptions must be handled or logged; an empty catch block that discards an error hides failures and security-relevant faults from operators.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An exception is caught and the catch block does nothing, so a failed integrity check, a partial write, or a security fault disappears with no log entry and no alert.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every catch block either recovers meaningfully or logs the exception with context, so no failure passes unobserved and operators can act on it.

[[C# PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
try { Process(); } catch (Exception) { }
```
Secure:
```
try { Process(); }
catch (Exception ex) { _logger.LogError(ex, "process failed"); }
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
try { riskyOperation() } catch (e) { }
```
Secure:
```
try { riskyOperation() } catch (e) { log.error("operation failed", e) }
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
try { parse(input); } catch (ParseException e) { }
```
Secure:
```
try { parse(input); }
catch (ParseException e) { log.error("parse failed", e); }
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
try { await save(); } catch (e) {}
```
Secure:
```
try { await save(); } catch (e) { logger.error("save failed", e); }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
try:
    process(record)
except Exception:
    pass
```
Secure:
```
try:
    process(record)
except Exception:
    logger.exception("record processing failed")
```
embed_text:
Caught exceptions must be handled or logged; an empty catch block that discards an error hides failures and security-relevant faults from operators.
An exception is caught and the catch block does nothing, so a failed integrity check, a partial write, or a security fault disappears with no log entry and no alert.
Every catch block either recovers meaningfully or logs the exception with context, so no failure passes unobserved and operators can act on it.
